petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-represented individual who filed an employment-based immigrant visa petition seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act. The decision provides minimal background on the petitioner's field or qualifications, mentioning only in passing that evidence submitted on motion referenced the petitioner being a co-owner of an entity and holding some position (details redacted). The petition was originally denied by the Director, Nebraska Service Center, on November 20, 2008, and the petitioner has pursued multiple unsuccessful appeals and motions since then.
outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the motion to reopen on multiple procedural grounds without reaching the merits of the extraordinary ability claim. First, the petitioner failed to submit a statement regarding whether the validity of the AAO's decision was the subject of any judicial proceeding as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(1)(iii), even after being placed on notice of this requirement. Second, the motion was untimely filed — received February 26, 2013, more than 21 months after the May 21, 2010 decision and more than four months after the October 9, 2012 rejection notice — and the petitioner failed to demonstrate that the delay was reasonable and beyond his control. Third, the petitioner failed to explain why the evidence submitted on motion (regarding co-ownership of an entity and another position) constituted 'new' facts that were not previously obtainable, as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(2). The AAO also denied the petitioner's request for oral argument, finding no unique factors or issues of law warranting it, and declined to reopen the matter on its own motion.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Multiple procedural issues led to dismissal without reaching the merits: (1) failure to submit the required statement regarding judicial proceedings under 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(1)(iii) despite prior notice; (2) untimely filing — the motion was received more than 21 months after the May 21, 2010 decision and four months after the October 9, 2012 rejection notice, well beyond the 33-day deadline; (3) the petitioner's claim that a prior late filing was due to errors by a hired filing service was unsupported, as the record showed the petitioner himself signed the Form I-290B received February 21, 2012; (4) failure to establish that submitted evidence constituted 'new facts' not previously obtainable under 8 C.F.R. § 103.5(a)(2); (5) the AAO had previously rejected two additional filings on December 13, 2011 and October 9, 2012.
